skill.name: image-outpainting
skill.description: Image outpainting on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Extends a still beyond its original canvas, fills in what the camera did not capture, and changes aspect ratio. Routes across multiple AI edit endpoints based on whether the outpaint is prose-driven, reference-driven, or brand-locked.
skill.summary: Extend images beyond their original canvas using RunComfy CLI. Change aspect ratios, uncrop portraits, and fill in missing areas with AI-powered outpainting.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: All 88 static analysis findings are FALSE POSITIVES when evaluated in context. The SKILL.md file is documentation for a legitimate image outpainting skill using the RunComfy CLI. The detected 'backtick execution' patterns are markdown fenced code blocks showing CLI usage examples. The 'hardcoded URLs' point to legitimate RunComfy and skills.sh domains. The 'hidden file access' at line 178 is documentation explaining token storage security practices. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'system reconnaissance' patterns are false triggers on model names and documentation text. The skill is well-documented with a dedicated security section and proper input handling guidelines.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution via runcomfy CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill invokes `runcomfy run` to perform image outpainting. This is the intended function of the skill. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The commands are explicitly documented in markdown code blocks with clear descriptions of what they do. The skill has a scoped Bash(runcomfy *) tool permission and documents shell injection prevention measures.

medium_findings.title: Network Requests to External API
medium_findings.description: The skill sends HTTP requests to runcomfy.net and runcomfy.com APIs. This is required for the image outpainting functionality. Confidence: 0.90. Confidence reasoning: Network requests are the core function of the skill - sending images to AI models for processing. All endpoints are documented and allowlisted.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Access for Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that the runcomfy CLI stores API tokens at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json. This is standard CLI authentication, not unauthorized file access. Confidence: 0.85. Confidence reasoning: The token storage path is documented in the Security section as a transparency measure, not an access instruction.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL in Example Code
low_findings.description: Example code block at line 86 contains a placeholder URL `https://your-cdn.example/portrait-1to1.jpg`. This is a documentation placeholder, not an actual data exfiltration target. Confidence: 0.95. Confidence reasoning: The URL format clearly indicates it is a placeholder example using standard documentation conventions.

content.user_title: Extend and uncrop images beyond their original canvas
content.value_statement: Images are often cropped to the wrong aspect ratio, losing context. This skill extends any image beyond its original boundaries by adding AI-generated content that matches the existing scene, lighting, and style.
actual_capabilities: Extend image canvas in any direction to change aspect ratio
Uncrop portraits to reveal more of the subject and background
Convert between aspect ratios such as 1:1, 16:9, 9:16, and 21:9
Route between multiple AI models for best outpainting quality
Preserve original image content while adding new surrounding areas
limitations: Cannot perform mask-driven inpaint edits inside the existing canvas area
Does not support video outpainting or extending video frames
May produce visible seams in complex boundary regions for some models
Requires the runcomfy CLI to be installed and authenticated

use_cases.title: Social media content creator
use_cases.description: Convert square product photos to 9:16 vertical format for Instagram Reels and TikTok without losing image quality.
use_cases.target_user: Social media manager or content creator

use_cases.title: Web designer
use_cases.description: Extend portrait-oriented photos to 21:9 widescreen format for hero banners and website headers.

use_cases.title: Photo restorer
use_cases.description: Restore cropped historical photos by extending the canvas with period-appropriate environment references.

prompt_templates.title: Basic aspect ratio change
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend this image to a 16:9 aspect ratio by adding matching environment on the left and right sides. Keep the original subject exactly as in the input.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user changing a square photo to widescreen

prompt_templates.title: Uncrop a portrait
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the canvas downward to show the subjects full upper body and arms. Continue the existing clothing style, lighting, and background. Keep the face and current visible area exactly as in the input.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to reveal more of a tightly cropped portrait

prompt_templates.title: Reference-based outpainting
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend this image to a 16:9 aspect ratio. Use the provided reference images to match the surrounding style. Add matching sky and architecture continuing from the existing scene.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user with style reference images for accurate extension

prompt_templates.title: Multi-step banner creation
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform sequential outpainting passes. First extend the canvas to the right by 30 percent. Use the result as input and extend to the left by 30 percent. Each pass should maintain quality and consistency.
prompt_templates.scenario: User creating a wide banner image through multiple extension steps

output_examples.input: User provides a square 1:1 portrait photo of a person in front of a building and asks to change it to 16:9 widescreen.
output: The skill generates a wider version of the image with the building continuing to the left and right
Additional sky and architectural details are added that match the original
The person and the original center portion remain exactly the same as the input

output_examples.input: User provides a tightly cropped headshot and asks to uncrop it to show more body.
output: The image is extended downward to reveal the subjects shoulders and upper body
Clothing style and color are continued from what is visible in the original
Background is extended downward to match the original environment

best_practices: Always include preservation language in prompts: state what must stay unchanged alongside what should be added
Set aspect_ratio explicitly in the JSON input to lock the output canvas dimensions
For multi-step outpainting, limit each pass to 30-50 percent extension to avoid quality degradation
anti_patterns: Omitting preservation instructions in the prompt, which may cause the model to regenerate original content
Relying on the AI model to guess the aspect ratio from text alone instead of setting it explicitly
Trying to perform detailed local inpaint edits that require masks instead of using a dedicated inpainting skill

faq.question: What is image outpainting?
faq.answer: Image outpainting extends an image beyond its original boundaries by generating new content that matches the existing scene.

faq.question: Does this skill change the original image content?
faq.answer: No. The skill is designed to preserve the original image content while adding new areas around it.

faq.question: What aspect ratios can I convert to?
faq.answer: You can convert to any aspect ratio. Common options include 16:9, 9:16, 21:9, and 4:3.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything?
faq.answer: Yes. The runcomfy CLI must be installed. Install with npm i -g @runcomfy/cli and sign in with runcomfy login.

faq.question: What is the difference between outpainting and inpainting?
faq.answer: Outpainting adds content outside the original canvas. Inpainting fills or replaces content inside the existing image.

faq.question: Can I use my own reference images?
faq.answer: Yes. Advanced models like GPT Image 2 Edit support reference images to match the extension style to your references.
